questions.question:
Context-free languages and regular languages are both closed under the operation(s) of:
A. Union
B. Intersection
C. Concatenation
D. Complementation
questions.to_find: Operations under which both context-free languages (CFL) and regular languages (RL) are closed

understand.plain: A class of languages is 'closed' under an operation if applying that operation to languages in the class always gives a language still in the class. This question asks which operations keep both CFLs and RLs within their respective classes. We need to find operations where BOTH classes are closed.

keywords.explain: A language class is closed under operation X if applying X to any languages in that class produces a language still in the class.
keywords.example: Regular languages are closed under union: if L1 and L2 are regular, then L1 union L2 is also regular.

keywords.explain: Languages generated by context-free grammars; recognized by pushdown automata. CFLs are closed under union and concatenation but NOT under intersection or complementation.
keywords.example: {a^n b^n | n>=0} is a CFL but not regular.

keywords.explain: Languages recognized by finite automata / described by regular expressions. Closed under all Boolean operations (union, intersection, complementation) and concatenation.
keywords.example: The language of strings over {a,b} containing 'ab' as substring is regular.

Complementation</text><rect x='135' y='180' width='160' height='32' rx='4' fill='#1D9E75' opacity='0.3'/><text x='215' y='201' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>YES (closed)</text><rect x='300' y='180' width='210' height='32' rx='4' fill='#D85A30' opacity='0.3'/><text x='405' y='201' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>NO (not closed)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing closure properties: both RL and CFL are closed under Union and Concatenation. RL is also closed under Intersection and Complementation, but CFL is NOT closed under Intersection or Complementation.

given.aim: Identify operations under which BOTH regular languages and context-free languages are closed.

terms.meaning: RLs are closed under union, intersection, complementation, concatenation, and Kleene star (all Boolean operations + concatenation-related).
terms.example: If L1 = {a*} and L2 = {b*} are regular, then L1 union L2 is also regular.
terms.connects: RL is closed under all four given operations.

terms.meaning: If L1 and L2 are CFLs, L1 union L2 is a CFL. Proven by creating a new start symbol S -> S1 | S2.
terms.connects: CFL is closed under union, so option A is valid.

terms.meaning: CFLs are NOT closed under intersection or complementation. Counterexample: {a^n b^n c^n} is not a CFL but can be expressed as intersection of two CFLs.
terms.example: {a^n b^n | n>=0} intersect {a^m b^m c^m | m>=0} = {a^n b^n c^n} which is not CFL.
terms.connects: CFL fails intersection and complementation, eliminating B and D.

terms.meaning: If L1 and L2 are CFLs, L1.L2 (concatenation) is also a CFL. CFGs for L1 and L2 can be combined.
terms.connects: Both RL and CFL are closed under concatenation, so option C is valid.

given.plan: Check each operation for both RL and CFL. RL is closed under all four. CFL is closed under union and concatenation but NOT under intersection and complementation. The answer is the intersection of what both are closed under.

steps.title: Check Union (A)
steps.apply: Regular languages: closed (union of two DFAs/NFAs gives an NFA). Context-free languages: closed (new grammar S -> S1 | S2). BOTH closed under union.
steps.note: This is a fundamental property taught early in automata theory.

steps.title: Check Intersection (B)
steps.apply: Regular languages: closed (product construction on DFAs). Context-free languages: NOT closed. Counterexample: L1 = {a^i b^j c^k | i=j} and L2 = {a^i b^j c^k | j=k} are both CFLs, but L1 intersect L2 = {a^n b^n c^n} is NOT a CFL.
steps.note: CFL fails intersection, so B is not valid for both classes.

steps.title: Check Concatenation (C)
steps.apply: Regular languages: closed (concatenation of NFAs). Context-free languages: closed (new grammar S -> S1 S2). BOTH closed under concatenation.
steps.note: Concatenation is a fundamental constructive operation for both classes.

steps.title: Check Complementation (D)
steps.apply: Regular languages: closed (swap accept/reject states in DFA). Context-free languages: NOT closed. By De Morgan's law, if CFL were closed under complement, it would be closed under intersection too (which it is not).
steps.note: CFL fails complementation, so D is not valid for both classes.

solution.result: A and C — Both regular and context-free languages are closed under Union and Concatenation.

questions.formula_note: Closure properties of language classes: RL is closed under all Boolean operations and concatenation; CFL is closed under union and concatenation but NOT under intersection or complementation.

questions.question:
Which of the following three statements are true? Prove your answer.
i.  The union of two recursive languages is recursive.
ii.  The language {0^n | n is a prime} is not regular.
iii. Regular languages are closed under infinite union.
questions.to_find: How many of the three given statements about recursive and regular languages are true

understand.plain: Three independent statements are given about formal languages. Statement i is about recursive languages being closed under union. Statement ii is about whether the set of strings 0^n where n is prime is regular. Statement iii claims regular languages are closed under arbitrarily infinite unions. We must determine how many of these are correct.

keywords.explain: A language L is recursive (decidable) if there exists a Turing machine that halts and accepts strings in L and halts and rejects strings not in L.
keywords.example: The language of all valid C programs that halt is not recursive, but the language of all palindromes over {a,b} is recursive.

keywords.explain: Any sufficiently long string in a regular language can be 'pumped' (a middle portion repeated any number of times) and remain in the language. Used to prove languages are NOT regular.
keywords.example: Apply pumping lemma to {a^n b^n}: pumping the first segment gives unequal a's and b's, contradiction.

keywords.explain: The union of infinitely many languages; even if each individual language is regular, the infinite union need not be regular.
keywords.example: Each {0^p} (a single string) is finite and hence regular, but the union over all primes p is {0^p | p is prime}, which is not regular.

understand.visual_alt: Three numbered statements to evaluate: (i) union of two recursive languages, (ii) set of 0^n for prime n, (iii) infinite union of regular languages.

given.aim: Count how many of the three statements (i), (ii), (iii) are true.

terms.term: Statement i: union of two recursive languages
terms.meaning: If L1 and L2 are recursive, is L1 union L2 also recursive?
terms.example: Both the language of even-length strings and odd-length strings over {a} are recursive. Their union (all strings over {a}) is also recursive.
terms.connects: Recursive languages are closed under union (run both TMs in parallel / simulate).

terms.meaning: The set of unary strings where the length is a prime number.
terms.connects: Use the pumping lemma to show this cannot be regular.

terms.term: Statement iii: infinite union of regular languages
terms.meaning: If L1, L2, L3, ... are all regular, is the union L1 union L2 union ... (infinitely many) also regular?
terms.example: Each {0^n} for a specific n is a finite (hence regular) language, but the union of infinitely many such singletons can be non-regular.
terms.connects: Regular languages are NOT closed under infinite union; statement iii is FALSE.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement independently: (i) use closure of decidable languages under union; (ii) apply pumping lemma; (iii) give a counterexample using infinite union of singleton regular languages yielding a non-regular language.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement i: Union of two recursive languages
steps.apply: Given TM M1 deciding L1 and TM M2 deciding L2, construct TM M: on input w, run M1 on w; if M1 accepts, accept. Else run M2 on w; if M2 accepts, accept; else reject. Both M1 and M2 always halt (since L1, L2 are recursive), so M always halts. Therefore L1 union L2 is recursive. Statement i is TRUE.
steps.note: This uses the closure of decidable/recursive languages under union.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement ii: {0^n | n is prime} is not regular
steps.formula_raw: Pumping lemma: if L regular, for |w| >= p, w = xyz with |xy| <= p, |y| >= 1, xy^k z in L for all k >= 0
steps.apply: Assume {0^n | n is prime} is regular with pumping length p. Choose w = 0^q where q is a prime >= p+2. Then w = xyz with |xy| <= p, |y| = m >= 1. Then xy^k z = 0^(q + (k-1)m) for k >= 0. For k = q+1: exponent = q + q*m = q(1+m). Since q >= 2 and (1+m) >= 2, q(1+m) is composite. So xy^(q+1)z is not in the language — contradiction. Statement ii is TRUE.
steps.note: The primes are too sparse for any finite automaton to track.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement iii: Regular languages closed under infinite union
steps.apply: Consider L_n = {0^n} for each fixed n. Each L_n is a finite (singleton) language, hence regular. But the infinite union over all primes p: union = {0^p | p is prime}, which we just proved is NOT regular. This is a direct counterexample. Statement iii is FALSE.
steps.note: Closure of regular languages under finite union follows from the closure of DFAs under product/NFA construction, but infinite union breaks this.

steps.title: Count true statements
steps.apply: Statement i: TRUE. Statement ii: TRUE. Statement iii: FALSE. Total true statements = 2.
steps.note: The answer is 2.

solution.result: 2 statements are true: statement i (union of two recursive languages is recursive) and statement ii ({0^n | n is prime} is not regular).

questions.formula_note: Pumping lemma for regular languages (to prove non-regularity) and closure of recursive languages under Boolean operations.

questions.question:
Which of the following is true?
A. The complement of a recursive language is recursive
B. The complement of a recursively enumerable language is recursively enumerable
C. The complement of a recursive language is either recursive or recursively enumerable
D. The complement of a context-free language is context-free
questions.answer: A. The complement of a recursive language is recursive
questions.to_find: Which statement about complements of language classes is true

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of what happens when you take the complement of languages in different classes (recursive, recursively enumerable, context-free). The complement of a language L over alphabet sigma is the set of all strings over sigma that are NOT in L. We need to identify the true closure property among the four options.

keywords.explain: A language L is recursive if there is a Turing machine that halts on ALL inputs, accepting strings in L and rejecting strings not in L.
keywords.example: Any regular, context-free, or context-sensitive language is recursive. Primality testing language {1^p | p is prime} is recursive.

keywords.explain: A language L is RE if there is a TM that accepts all strings in L (may loop forever on strings not in L). Every recursive language is RE, but not vice versa.
keywords.example: The halting problem is RE but not recursive.

keywords.explain: The complement of an RE language. If L is RE but not recursive, then its complement is co-RE but not RE.
keywords.example: The complement of the halting problem is co-RE but not RE.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='200' viewBox='0 0 480 200' UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Complement Closure</text><rect x='10' y='35' width='200' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Recursive (decidable)</text><rect x='220' y='35' width='250' height='30' rx='4' fill='#1D9E75' opacity='0.3' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='1'/><text x='345' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>complement = Recursive (TRUE)</text><rect x='10' y='75' width='200' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>RE (not recursive)</text><rect x='220' y='75' width='250' height='30' rx='4' fill='#D85A30' opacity='0.3' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='1'/><text x='345' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>complement = co-RE (NOT RE)</text><rect x='10' y='115' width='200' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Context-Free (CFL)</text><rect x='220' y='115' width='250' height='30' rx='4' fill='#D85A30' opacity='0.3' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='1'/><text x='345' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>complement may not be CFL</text><rect x='10' y='155' width='200' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Regular</text><rect x='220' y='155' width='250' height='30' rx='4' fill='#1D9E75' opacity='0.3' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='1'/><text x='345' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>complement = Regular (closed)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table: Recursive -> complement is Recursive (true). RE (not recursive) -> complement is co-RE, not RE. CFL -> complement may not be CFL. Regular -> complement is Regular.

given.aim: Determine which statement about complements of language classes is correct.

terms.term: Option A: complement of recursive is recursive
terms.meaning: If TM M decides L (halts on all inputs), then a TM M' that swaps accept/reject states of M decides complement(L). Both halt on all inputs.
terms.example: If M accepts even-length strings and rejects odd-length strings, then M' with swapped states accepts odd-length strings.
terms.connects: This is TRUE — recursive languages are closed under complementation.

terms.term: Option B: complement of RE is RE
terms.meaning: This is FALSE. If L is RE but not recursive, complement(L) is co-RE but NOT RE. If both L and complement(L) were RE, then L would be recursive.
terms.example: Halting problem is RE. Complement of halting problem is NOT RE.

terms.term: Option D: complement of CFL is CFL
terms.meaning: CFLs are NOT closed under complementation. The complement of a CFL may not be context-free.
terms.example: {a^n b^n c^n} is not a CFL, but it can be expressed as the complement of a CFL.

given.plan: For each option, apply the definition of the language class and its closure/non-closure under complementation. Option A is the well-known fact that decidable languages are closed under complement.

steps.title: Verify Option A: complement of recursive is recursive
steps.apply: Let M be a TM deciding L. Construct M': on input w, simulate M on w. If M accepts, M' rejects. If M rejects, M' accepts. Since M always halts, M' always halts. M' decides complement(L). Therefore complement of recursive language is recursive. Option A is TRUE.
steps.note: This is the key proof: simply swapping accept and reject states in a deciding TM.

steps.title: Verify Option B: complement of RE is RE
steps.formula_raw: If L and complement(L) both RE => L recursive
steps.apply: This is FALSE. By Rice's theorem consequences and direct proof: if L is RE but not recursive, then complement(L) is NOT RE. Proof: if both L and complement(L) were RE (with TMs M1 and M2), run M1 and M2 in parallel on input w; one must halt, giving a decider for L — contradicting L not being recursive.
steps.note: The complement of the halting problem is the canonical counterexample.

steps.title: Eliminate options C and D
steps.apply: Option C says complement of recursive is either recursive or RE. But A already says it's always recursive (a stronger and correct statement). Option D says complement of CFL is CFL — FALSE since CFLs are not closed under complementation. The correct answer is A.
steps.note: Option C is weaker than A and technically vacuously true, but A is the precise/correct complete answer expected by GATE.

solution.result: A. The complement of a recursive language is recursive.

questions.formula_note: Recursive languages are closed under complementation: swap accept/reject in a deciding TM. RE languages are NOT closed under complementation (if L and co-L are both RE, then L is recursive).

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is/are FALSE?
1. For every non-deterministic Turing machine, there exists an equivalent deterministic Turing machine.
2. Turing recognizable languages are closed under union and complementation.
3. Turing decidable languages are closed under intersection and complementation.
4. Turing recognizable languages are closed under union and intersection.
A. 2 and 4
B. 1 and 3
C. 2 only
D. 3 only
questions.to_find: Which of the four statements about Turing machines and language classes are FALSE

understand.plain: We are given four statements about Turing machines and closure properties of Turing recognizable (RE) and Turing decidable (recursive) languages. We need to find which statements are false. Key knowledge: RE languages are closed under union and intersection but NOT under complementation; recursive languages are closed under all Boolean operations.

keywords.explain: A language accepted by some Turing machine that accepts strings in the language (may loop on strings not in the language). Also called semi-decidable or recursively enumerable.
keywords.example: The halting problem: {<M,w> | M halts on w} is Turing recognizable.

keywords.explain: A language accepted by a TM that always halts (accepts members, rejects non-members). A proper subset of RE languages.
keywords.example: The language of all regular expressions that accept at least one string is decidable.

keywords.explain: For every non-deterministic TM there is an equivalent deterministic TM (recognizes the same language). This is proven by simulating NTM with a DTM using BFS over configurations.
keywords.example: NP problems can be solved by a DTM (in exponential time), showing NTM and DTM are equivalent in computability (not necessarily complexity).

understand.visual_alt: Four statements to classify as TRUE or FALSE: NTM=DTM power (TRUE), RE closed under complementation (FALSE), Recursive closed under intersection and complement (TRUE), RE closed under union and intersection (TRUE).

given.aim: Find which statements among the four are FALSE.

terms.meaning: Every NTM can be simulated by a DTM. Both have the same computational power (they recognize the same class of languages).
terms.example: BFS simulation: DTM explores all non-deterministic branches level by level.

terms.term: Statement 2: RE closed under union and complementation
terms.meaning: RE is closed under union: if L1, L2 are RE, L1 union L2 is RE (dovetail two TMs). RE is NOT closed under complementation: complement of RE may not be RE.
terms.example: Complement of halting problem is not RE.
terms.connects: The 'and complementation' part makes statement 2 FALSE.

terms.term: Statement 3: Recursive closed under intersection and complementation
terms.meaning: Recursive (decidable) languages are closed under ALL Boolean operations including intersection (run both deciders, accept iff both accept) and complementation (swap accept/reject).
terms.example: Intersection of two decidable languages: run M1 then M2; accept iff both accept.

terms.term: Statement 4: RE closed under union and intersection
terms.meaning: RE is closed under union (dovetail TMs) and intersection (dovetail TMs, accept iff both accept). Both operations preserve the RE property.
terms.example: Union: on input w, run M1 and M2 in parallel; accept if either accepts.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement. RE is closed under union and intersection but NOT complementation. Recursive is closed under all Boolean operations. NTM = DTM in computational power. Identify the false statement.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement 1
steps.apply: For every NTM N, construct DTM D that simulates N using BFS over all computation paths. D accepts iff N has at least one accepting path. Standard theorem in computability theory. Statement 1 is TRUE.
steps.note: NTM does not give extra computational power over DTM (only possibly extra speed in complexity).

steps.title: Evaluate Statement 2
steps.apply: RE closed under union: TRUE (dovetail two TMs). RE closed under complementation: FALSE. If L is RE but not recursive, complement(L) is not RE. The statement claims RE is closed under BOTH union AND complementation. Since the complementation part is false, statement 2 as a whole is FALSE.
steps.note: This is the key false statement.

steps.title: Evaluate Statements 3 and 4
steps.apply: Statement 3: Recursive closed under intersection (run M1 then M2) and complementation (swap states in decider) — both TRUE. Statement 3 is TRUE. Statement 4: RE closed under union (run M1 || M2, accept if either accepts) and intersection (run M1 || M2, accept only if both accept) — both TRUE. Statement 4 is TRUE.
steps.note: Recursive = decidable is the most robust class, closed under all Boolean operations.

steps.title: Identify the answer
steps.apply: Only statement 2 is FALSE. Answer = C (2 only).
steps.note: Option A says '2 and 4' — wrong since 4 is true. Option C says '2 only' — correct.

solution.result: C. 2 only — Statement 2 is false because Turing recognizable languages are NOT closed under complementation (they are closed under union and intersection, but not complementation).

questions.formula_note: RE languages closed under union and intersection but NOT complementation. Recursive (decidable) languages closed under all Boolean operations including complementation.

questions.question:
Consider the following types of languages:
L_1: Regular, L_2: Context-free, L_3: Recursive, L_4: Recursively enumerable.
Which of the following statements is TRUE?
A. Only L_1 and L_2 are closed under complementation
B. Only L_1 and L_3 are closed under complementation
C. Only L_2 and L_4 are closed under complementation
D. All four are closed under complementation
questions.answer: B. Only L_1 and L_3 are closed under complementation
questions.to_find: Which of the four language classes (Regular, CFL, Recursive, RE) are closed under complementation

understand.plain: We have four language classes in a hierarchy: Regular subset CFL subset Recursive subset RE. This question asks which of these four classes are closed under complementation (i.e., if L is in the class, then the complement of L must also be in the class). The answer involves knowing that Regular and Recursive are closed under complementation, but CFL and RE are not.

keywords.explain: For language L over alphabet sigma, complement(L) = sigma* - L = all strings over sigma that are NOT in L.
keywords.example: If L = {strings with even number of a's}, then complement(L) = {strings with odd number of a's}.

keywords.explain: A language class C is closed under complementation if for every L in C, complement(L) is also in C.
keywords.example: Regular languages: if L is recognized by DFA M, swap accept/reject states to get DFA for complement(L). So RL is closed.

keywords.explain: CFLs recognized by deterministic PDA. DCFLs ARE closed under complementation, but the general class of CFLs is not closed under complementation.
keywords.example: The language {a^n b^n c^n} is not CFL. Its complement is a CFL, showing CFLs are not closed under complementation.

text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>NOT CLOSED</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table of four language classes and their complementation closure: Regular = closed (DFA state swap), CFL = NOT closed, Recursive = closed (TM state swap), RE = NOT closed.

given.aim: Identify which language classes among Regular, CFL, Recursive, RE are closed under complementation.

terms.meaning: Given DFA M for L, swap all accepting and non-accepting states to get DFA M' for complement(L). DFA is deterministic and complete, so this always works.
terms.example: DFA for {strings ending in 'a'}: complement is DFA for {strings not ending in 'a'}.

terms.term: L_2 CFL: NOT closed under complementation
terms.meaning: PDA is non-deterministic; swapping accept/reject states does NOT give a PDA for the complement. Proof: complement of CFL may not be CFL.
terms.example: L = {a^i b^j c^k | i != j or j != k} is CFL. But complement = {a^n b^n c^n} is not CFL.
terms.connects: L_2 is NOT closed under complementation.

terms.meaning: If TM M decides L (halts on all inputs), swap accept/reject in M to decide complement(L). Since M always halts, M' always halts too.
terms.example: Deciding {w | |w| is even} — swap to decide {w | |w| is odd}.

terms.term: L_4 RE: NOT closed under complementation
terms.meaning: If L is RE but not recursive, complement(L) may not be RE. If both L and complement(L) were RE, L would be recursive.
terms.example: Halting problem is RE; its complement is not RE.
terms.connects: L_4 is NOT closed under complementation.

given.plan: For each class, check if the construction for complement (swap accept/reject) works. It works for DFA (Regular) and deciding TM (Recursive) because they are deterministic and always halt. It fails for NFA/PDA (CFL) and accepting-only TM (RE).

steps.title: Regular (L_1): closed under complementation
steps.formula_raw: DFA M' = (Q, sigma, delta, q_0, Q - F)
steps.apply: For any DFA M = (Q, sigma, delta, q_0, F) accepting L, construct M' = (Q, sigma, delta, q_0, Q - F) by swapping accepting and non-accepting states. M' accepts exactly complement(L). This works because DFA is deterministic and complete (every string reaches exactly one state).
steps.note: This works ONLY for DFA, not NFA (incomplete transitions would need completion first).

steps.title: CFL (L_2): NOT closed under complementation
steps.apply: Counterexample: L1 = {a^i b^j c^k | i=j} (CFL), L2 = {a^i b^j c^k | j=k} (CFL). complement(L1) union complement(L2) = complement(L1 intersect L2) by De Morgan = complement({a^n b^n c^n}). If CFL were closed under complementation and union, then {a^n b^n c^n} would be CFL — contradiction. Hence CFL is NOT closed.
steps.note: CFL is not closed under complementation (nor under intersection).

steps.title: Recursive (L_3): closed under complementation
steps.formula_raw: M' accepts w iff M rejects w
steps.apply: Let M be a TM that decides L. Construct M' that simulates M and swaps accept/reject. Since M always halts on every input, M' also always halts. M' decides complement(L). So Recursive is closed under complementation.
steps.note: Key difference from RE: the TM must HALT on all inputs (not just accept).

steps.title: RE (L_4): NOT closed under complementation
steps.apply: The halting problem H = {<M,w> | M halts on w} is RE. Suppose complement(H) = {<M,w> | M does NOT halt on w} were also RE. Then both H and complement(H) are RE, which would make H recursive (decidable). But H is undecidable — contradiction. So RE is NOT closed under complementation.
steps.note: This is one of the most important results in computability theory.

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.apply: Closed: L_1 (Regular) and L_3 (Recursive). Not closed: L_2 (CFL) and L_4 (RE). Answer = B.
steps.note: The pattern: classes defined by deterministic models that always halt are closed under complementation.

solution.result: B. Only L_1 (Regular) and L_3 (Recursive) are closed under complementation.

questions.formula_note: Complementation closure: works for Regular (DFA state swap) and Recursive (deciding TM state swap), but fails for CFL (non-deterministic PDA) and RE (accepting-only TM may loop).
